documents.content: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces, created by Jordan Walke at Facebook in 2013 and now maintained by Meta. It uses a component-based architecture where UIs are composed of small, reusable pieces. The virtual DOM is React's key performance innovation: instead of manipulating the browser DOM directly, React maintains an in-memory representation and computes the minimal set of changes needed. To start learning React in 2025, begin with the new official documentation at react.dev which teaches functional components and hooks from day one. Avoid older tutorials that teach class components as the primary pattern—they're legacy now. Plan on 2-3 weeks to become productive if you already know JavaScript well.

Essential React hooks to master, in order: useState for component-local state, useEffect for side effects and lifecycle behavior, useContext for cross-component state without prop drilling, useRef for DOM access and mutable values that don't trigger renders, useMemo and useCallback for performance optimization (don't reach for these until you have measured a problem), useReducer for complex state logic. Common beginner mistakes: putting too much logic in useEffect, calling hooks conditionally, forgetting dependencies in the dependency array, mutating state directly instead of replacing it. Use the React DevTools browser extension and the eslint-plugin-react-hooks rule to catch these mistakes early.

Recommended React learning roadmap: Week 1—JavaScript fundamentals review (destructuring, spread, async/await, modules). Week 2—JSX, components, props, basic state with useState. Week 3—useEffect, conditional rendering, lists and keys, forms. Week 4—routing with React Router, data fetching, error boundaries. Week 5—state management options (Context, Zustand, Redux Toolkit). Week 6—testing with React Testing Library and Vitest, accessibility, deployment. After this, branch into ecosystem tools like Next.js (full-stack React with SSR) or React Native (mobile). Free resources: react.dev official tutorial, FreeCodeCamp, Scrimba's React course. Paid but worth it: Kent C. Dodds' Epic React.
